hypothesis.text: ADHD eye-tracking screening can replace clinical interviews for diagnosing attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder in children.

argument.text: Eye-tracking technology provides objective, quantitative measures of attentional function that are free from rater bias. Studies have shown that machine learning classifiers based on eye-tracking features can achieve 80-85% accuracy in discriminating ADHD from control participants. This accuracy is comparable to the inter-rater reliability of structured clinical interviews (kappa = 0.70-0.80), suggesting that eye-tracking screening could serve as a standalone diagnostic tool, potentially replacing the need for time-consuming and resource-intensive clinical interviews.
supporting_evidence: Deng, S., Zhang, Y., & Cheng, H. (2020). A machine learning approach for ADHD classification using eye-tracking data. Scientific Reports, 10, 21849.
Munoz, D. P., Armstrong, I. T., & Everling, S. (2023). Oculomotor abnormalities in ADHD: A systematic review and meta-analysis. Biological Psychiatry.
